query: Chicken pox infection risk in pregnant women and its impact on fetus

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Varicella Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The seronegative patients are at risk of acquiring VZV during pregnancy which is associated with a higher mortality rate due to developing varicella pneumonitis. Possible fetal effects of varicella may also occur and include fetal demise or manifestations of either congenital varicella syndrome (embryopathy) or neonatal varicella (development of chickenpox or disseminated disease) the first 10 days of life), which are associated with significant morbidity and mortality. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Varicella Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Varicella infection during pregnancy is uncommon due to widespread immunity in women of childbearing age after having chickenpox or varicella immunization which was introduced in 1995. Varicella infection in a seronegative pregnant female occurs if she acquires person-to-person transmission from direct contact with varicella-zoster lesions or respiratory droplets spread from an infected person with chickenpox. Clinical presentation and the severity of the neonate will depend on when the mother contracted the virus during the pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Etiology -- Illness Acquired Via Airborne Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV), or human herpesvirus 3, is the etiologic agent of chickenpox; reactivation of VZV causes shingles. VZV primarily spreads through respiratory droplets or direct contact with blister fluid. Primary infection with VZV during pregnancy is now rare secondary to vaccination; individuals of childbearing age have a high seroprevalence, especially in the United States. [7] [34] Primary VZV infection during pregnancy, rather than secondary infection or reactivation, is believed to lead to congenital varicella syndrome. [35] Vertical transmission of VZV is thought to be transplacental; the mechanism is unknown. Primary acquisition of VZV by pregnant individuals before an estimated gestational age of 20 weeks poses the highest risk of subsequent congenital varicella syndrome (0.91%). Congenital varicella syndrome has been reported following maternal primary varicella infection between an estimated gestational age of 20 and 28 weeks and recently as late as 36 weeks. [36] [37] Please see StatPearls' companion reference, " Congenital Varicella Syndrome ," for additional information.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Vertical transplacental infections pose a significant risk in maternal-fetal and pediatric health, as various pathogens can cross the placental barrier and adversely affect the developing fetus. These infections include those caused by viruses such as rubella, cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex, parvovirus B19, hepatitis C, varicella-zoster, and Zika, as well as other pathogens like Toxoplasma gondii , Listeria monocytogenes , and Treponema pallidum . The outcomes of these infections vary, ranging from mild illness to severe congenital anomalies, fetal death, or chronic conditions that persist into infancy. The timing of the antenatal infection, along with the specific pathogen involved, greatly influences the severity and type of outcomes, necessitating precise and timely interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicella (Chickenpox) Vaccine -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients can not receive the vaccine if they present with febrile illness or have active, untreated tuberculosis. Vaccination is contraindicated in pregnant females, and women should delay pregnancy for 3 months after vaccination by using effective birth control. Maternal varicella infection has been shown to harm the fetus, but the vaccination effects have not been tested on pregnant women, and the effects on fetal development are currently unknown. It is also not known whether the varicella vaccine virus passes in breast milk, and it is best to avoid vaccination during breastfeeding for that reason. There is currently no clinical data available on the efficacy or the safety of administration of the varicella vaccine in children younger than 12 months old.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicella-Zoster Virus (Chickenpox)  -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Chickenpox is more severe in pregnant women than in adults, posing 3 dangers to the unborn child: congenital varicella syndrome, possibly severe varicella, and herpes zoster. In cases of VZV exposure in a nonimmunized pregnant woman, prophylaxis entails the administration of anti-VZV immunoglobulins within 10 days post-exposure (0.5 to 1 ml/kg, IV). Maternal chickenpox occurring within 7 days before or after delivery might result in severe neonatal chickenpox; this necessitates the prompt introduction of anti-VZV immunoglobulins to the newborn within a maximum of 10 days post-birth. The timeframe should be prolonged to 21 days before delivery for infants delivered before 28 weeks of amenorrhea or weighing <1000 g at birth. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Varicella Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Because the effect on fetal development is not known, the varicella vaccine, which is a live vaccine, is contraindicated in pregnancy. Vaccination can be given in the immediate post-partum period, and breastfeeding is not a contraindication. [6] Prevention of VZV infection through vaccination is key to the prevention of neonatal complications. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Evaluation -- Varicella Virus
retrieved_docs.content: Acute varicella viral skin infection, or chickenpox—in an individual of any age, pregnant or otherwise—is usually diagnosed clinically based on the finding of a classic pruritic, vesicular rash. However, if laboratory diagnosis is desired, a sample may be taken from an unroofed lesion for qualitative varicella PCR. Subsequently, fetal varicella infection can be evaluated by ultrasonography after documented acute maternal infection. [52] Congenital varicella syndrome criteria include the appearance of chickenpox during pregnancy, the presence of congenital skin lesions in a dermatomal distribution or neurologic defects, eye disease, limb hypoplasia, proof of intrauterine VZV infection by detection of viral deoxyribonucleic acid in the fetus, the presence of VZV-specific IgM or IgG beyond 7 months of age, and the appearance of zoster during early infancy. [37] [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Deterrence of transplacental infections starts with comprehensive patient education on avoiding exposures that could lead to such infections. (Please refer to the Etiology section for more information on infection prevention.) Clinicians should emphasize the significance of regular prenatal care and follow-up, which allows for early detection and management of potential infections. Vaccination advice for preventable diseases, such as rubella and varicella, is a critical component of preconceptual counseling and care. Through proactive patient education and preventive measures, clinicians can significantly reduce the risk of transplacental infections, protecting maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Varicella Syndrome -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: The long-term side effects of VZIG and acyclovir use in the perinatal period have not been well established at this time. However,  the benefits of mitigating severe and possibly life-threatening infections outweigh the theoretical risk. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Varicella-Zoster Virus (Chickenpox)  -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant women are at an elevated risk of developing severe varicella, particularly pneumonia. Moreover, maternal varicella accompanied by viremia can be transmitted transplacentally to the fetus. This results in newborn varicella. Infants born between 5 days before and 2 days after the onset of the varicella rash in the mother are at the highest risk for serious illness. [6] The first month of a neonate's life is a vulnerable phase for severe varicella, particularly if the mother is seronegative. Delivery before 28 weeks of gestation predisposes a neonate to risk, as transplacental transfer of IgG antibodies occurs following this period.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Varicella Virus
retrieved_docs.content: If varicella infection is suspected or confirmed in a pregnant individual or their fetus, oral acyclovir, when started within 24 hours of developing the varicella rash, can decrease the duration of symptoms and the number of lesions that may develop. [52] Intravenous acyclovir may also reduce maternal complications associated with varicella pneumonia. However, neither route of acyclovir seems to have a significant effect on reducing the fetal effects of congenital varicella syndrome. [52] Pregnant patients exposed to varicella infection without evidence of immunity should be offered varicella immunoglobulin (VZIG) within 10 days of exposure. [52] [82] Despite sparse evidence, VZIG is believed to reduce the incidence of congenital varicella syndrome. [52] After birth, acyclovir and VZIG can be used to reduce perinatal transmission opportunities.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Varicella Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Since vaccination is the key to preventing neonatal varicella infection complications, all clinicians should urge their patients who are not immunocompromised or pregnant to receive two doses of the varicella vaccine if they have not already had chickenpox. Clinicians should educate pregnant women who are seronegative for VZV to avoid contact with active varicella infection individuals. A team of specialists should manage pregnant women who develop chickenpox to determine treatment for them and their neonates. Pharmacists can assist with immunoglobulin therapy, and nurses will guide administration. Maternal herpes zoster, however, poses little risk of neonatal complications or CVS due to the established circulating maternal antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicella-Zoster Immune Globulin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: While the demand for varicella-zoster immune globulin has reduced significantly, it is essential to remember that exposure to varicella and herpes zoster can still occur. Early recognition of exposure is critical in pregnant females as there is a high risk of maternal complications due to varicella infection. Using varicella-zoster immune globulin helps modify disease and reduces maternal morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicella-Zoster Virus (Chickenpox)  -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal varicella in the initial 20 weeks of gestation is linked to an approximately 2% incidence of congenital varicella syndrome (varicella embryopathy). Potential congenital anomalies encompass low birth weight, cutaneous scarring, ocular abnormalities, cortical atrophy, psychomotor delay, and hypoplastic limbs. Children whose mothers contracted varicella during gestation may get zoster early in life without ever experiencing extrauterine varicella. Severe neonatal varicella, characterized by the absence of maternal antibody protection, may arise when maternal varicella manifests between 5 days before and 2 days following delivery. Maternal herpes zoster, on the other hand, constitutes little risk of neonatal complications or congenital varicella syndrome, probably because of established circulating maternal antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Varicella Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal VZV immune status should be determined before pregnancy by reviewing the varicella infection history or vaccination documentation with two doses of the varicella vaccine. If both are negative, a VZV IgG should be obtained. Women of childbearing age without immunity should be counseled about the risks of varicella during pregnancy. Women of childbearing age who are not immune and not pregnant should receive two doses of the vaccine, given four to eight weeks apart.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2117
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy encompass a range of maternal conditions that can affect pregnancy outcomes. Among these, قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum) is identified as a specific child condition within the broader category of other pregnancy complications. Additionally, the category is classified to include الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), highlighting the impact of substance use on maternal and fetal health. The classification also covers الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), emphasizing the importance of infectious disease management in pregnant patients. Recognizing these interrelated conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy aids clinicians in comprehensive risk assessment, targeted prevention, and appropriate therapeutic interventions to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy constitute a broad category that includes several specific conditions. الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery) is classified as an other complication of pregnancy, highlighting the impact of substance use on maternal and fetal health. الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy) is also classified under other complications of pregnancy, emphasizing the risks of rubella infection to the developing fetus. Additionally, other complications of pregnancy has a child entity الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي (congenital herpes), indicating that congenital herpes is considered a specific manifestation within this group. Recognizing these conditions as part of other complications of pregnancy supports comprehensive prenatal assessment, risk stratification, and targeted interventions to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy represent a category of gestational health issues that includes قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة, a condition characterized by mild nausea or vomiting during pregnancy, and الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي, which refers to herpes infection occurring in the pregnant patient. This group of complications is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة, indicating that drug dependence or substance use prior to delivery is considered a related factor within the spectrum of other pregnancy complications. Recognizing these specific conditions and their classification helps clinicians address a broader range of maternal health concerns and tailor preventive and therapeutic strategies accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that includes several distinct conditions. It is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery). Within this category are the specific complications zwangerschap; vermoeidheid (pregnancy‑related fatigue) and طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). These entities represent various adverse events that can occur during gestation, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and management of substance use, fatigue, and dermatologic manifestations to optimize maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 786
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal pregnancy complications that are not classified elsewhere include several distinct conditions. Mild hyperemesis gravidarum (قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة) represents a less severe form of pregnancy‑related nausea and vomiting. German measles occurring during pregnancy (الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل) poses a risk to both mother and fetus and requires careful monitoring. Thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period (خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل) can affect maternal metabolism and fetal development, necessitating endocrine evaluation and treatment. Gestational herpes infection (الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي) is another maternal complication that may lead to adverse outcomes if not managed appropriately. Finally, anemia during pregnancy (فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل) is a common hematologic disorder that can compromise maternal health and fetal growth. Together, these conditions constitute a category of pregnancy‑related maternal complications that are recognized but not placed within more specific classification groups, highlighting the need for individualized assessment and management in prenatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1749
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة, الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل, and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل. These represent drug dependence before delivery, rubella infection (German measles) occurring during gestation, and thyroid dysfunction arising in the course of pregnancy, respectively. Recognizing these conditions as part of other complications of pregnancy underscores the importance of assessing substance use, preventing and managing rubella infection, and monitoring thyroid function to protect maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Mild hyperemesis gravidarum, thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy, and gestational herpes are all specific maternal complications that fall under the broader, uncategorized group of pregnancy complications affecting the mother. Each of these conditions represents a distinct health issue that can arise during gestation and requires appropriate clinical attention despite not being classified within more specific subcategories of maternal pregnancy disorders. Recognizing them as part of the general category of unclassified maternal pregnancy complications helps clinicians consider a wider range of potential problems when evaluating and managing pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.
